ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese ngapi (shrimp paste) rice. FOODON_00002675 (fried rice food product) is used as the closest parent category. The dish is a stir-fried rice preparation seasoned with fermented shrimp paste (ngapi/balachan) and topped with a fried egg.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Burmese balachan/ngapi rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese balachan rice or ngapi rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of balachan rice with fried egg is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 250 g of stir-fried rice and 100 g of fried egg, as typically served at a Burmese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried rice with egg — nutrient composition estimated from component analysis using USDA FoodData Central (cooked white rice, fried egg, cooking oil, shrimp paste) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese balachan rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from a component-based approach: cooked white rice (~65% by weight, USDA FDC #168878), fried egg (~25% by weight, USDA FDC #173424), cooking oil (~5% by weight, USDA FDC #171413), and ngapi shrimp paste (~5% by weight, FAO/INFOODS SEA). The no-meat variant excludes any meat contribution. Sodium is elevated due to ngapi (fermented shrimp paste), which is a high-sodium condiment. Values represent a plausible central estimate for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled/fried rice composite) and fried rice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese balachan rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, GI approximately 64, medium category). The dish is predominantly white rice stir-fried with minimal additions; the shrimp paste and egg do not substantially alter the glycemic response of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 24.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 55.7 g GL per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented shrimp paste (ngapi)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid balachan rice due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1470 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced shrimp paste or a low-sodium preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume balachan rice cautiously, as the white rice base carries a medium glycemic index (GI ~64) and a high glycemic load per serving (~55.7 g GL per 350 g plate), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction and pairing with non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol from shrimp paste and fried egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the combined sodium load (~1470 mg per serving) and dietary cholesterol (~298 mg per serving from the fried egg) in balachan rice, and should limit portion size or frequency of consumption in line with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Balachan Rice with Fried Egg (No Meat) is a Burmese rice dish in which cooked white rice is stir-fried with ngapi (ငပိ), a pungent fermented shrimp paste that is a cornerstone of Burmese cuisine, and served topped with a pan-fried egg. The dish is known in Burmese as ငပိထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ် (nga-pi htamin). The term 'balachan' is a Singaporean and Malay-influenced romanisation of the same fermented shrimp paste condiment (belacan in Malay). In this no-meat variant, the protein comes entirely from the fried egg rather than any added chicken, pork, or seafood. The rice is typically stir-fried in oil with garlic and onion before the ngapi is incorporated, imparting a deeply savoury, umami-rich, and slightly pungent flavour profile. The texture is soft and slightly sticky from the rice, with a crispy-edged fried egg on top. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, carbohydrate-dominant meal (~168 kcal per 100 g, ~24.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with moderate protein (~5.8 g per 100 g) from the egg. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) attributable to the fermented shrimp paste, making it a high-sodium dish that warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 64 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is high (~55.7 g), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish allergies due to the shrimp paste. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, this dish is served at Burmese restaurants such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese balachan rice exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from a component-based approach using USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for the constituent ingredients (cooked white rice, fried egg, cooking oil, shrimp paste). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (fried rice, white rice base) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a direct source becomes available. 2. Sodium content is a key clinical concern: ngapi (fermented shrimp paste) is a very high-sodium ingredient; the estimated 420 mg/100 g sodium figure may vary significantly depending on the quantity of shrimp paste used by individual restaurants. 3. The food_name has been expanded from the input 'Balachan Rice' to 'Balachan Rice with Fried Egg (No Meat)' to reflect the dish description in the original Burmese name (ငပိထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်), which explicitly includes fried egg (ကြက်ဥကြော်). 4. The cuisine_tags in the input ('Noodle', 'Lor Mee') appear to be erroneous field survey platform tags unrelated to this dish; they have been disregarded. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish uses standard cooking oil without industrial hydrogenation; confirmed zero per USDA FDC source data for the oil component. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2), potassium_mg (95.0), calcium_mg (18.0), iron_mg (0.6), cholesterol_mg (85.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.08), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.3), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.5), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (90.0), magnesium_mg (14.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (8.0), water_g (62.0) are all estimated from component-based calculation using USDA FDC data for cooked white rice, fried egg, and cooking oil. 7. is_halal is set to false as ngapi (fermented shrimp paste) may contain non-halal processing agents depending on the producer; halal status cannot be confirmed without restaurant-specific certification. 8. GI of 64 places this dish in the medium category (56–69 inclusive) per the Veritas GI threshold rules.
